originalTitle: Prosecutors say video shows suspect in Charlie Kirk killing climbing on to roof
summary: Utah county attorney’s office says evidence against Tyler James Robinson is sufficient for case to move to trial
content: Utah prosecutors showed video footage on Tuesday allegedly depicting Tyler James Robinson entering the campus of Utah Valley University on the day of Charlie Kirk’s killing and climbing on to a roof from where they say he fired a fatal shot at the conservative commentator. The Utah county attorney’s office presented the evidence on the second day of a week-long preliminary hearing in which prosecutors must prove to state district judge Tony Graf that there was enough evidence for the case to move to trial. Robinson, 23, has been charged with aggravated murder in the killing of Kirk, who died after being shot in the neck while speaking at the Utah university last September. Prosecutors are seeking the death penalty against Robinson, and say there is DNA evidence that links him to the weapon believed to be used in the killing. The state has also said Robinson allegedly confessed in a note he left his roommate and romantic partner. Robinson has not yet entered a plea in the case. The proceedings this week marked the first time Kirk’s family and his widow, Erika Kirk, have been in the courtroom with his accused killer. Donald Trump Jr, a close friend of Kirk’s, was also in attendance on Monday. On the first day of the hearing, Christopher Bagley, a former Utah Valley University police officer, described hearing a gunshot while Kirk was speaking to thousands of people on 10 September 2025, and the panic that ensued. “Everybody kind of got up. A lot of people were screaming and starting to run in all different directions,” Bagley told the court. He searched for additional victims, but finding none, moved to contain the scene to preserve evidence, he said. Shortly after, Bagley inspected the rooftop of a nearby building and saw impressions in the gravel that he believed suggested a sniper had been there. The prosecution also submitted graphic footage of the shooting itself into evidence that was not shown to the public. Tuesday’s proceedings began with David Hull, a former Utah state bureau of investigation agent, taking the stand. Hull narrated what was unfolding in videos played in court that he said showed Robinson arriving on campus, leaving and then returning later in different clothing. Additional footage showed a person authorities believe to be Robinson entering a rooftop, running and then crouching down and lying prone until a shot was fired at 12.23, Hull said, at which point the person got up, lowered down from the roof and walked away from campus. The prosecution also showed footage that Hull said showed Robinson’s vehicle on campus in the early morning hours of 11 September. A police officer interacted with the driver of the vehicle, Hull said, and in an act of “cop intuition” wrote down details about the exchange and a partial license plate number. After searching the number, authorities determined Robinson was an owner of the vehicle. The prosecution and defense have clashed over additional videos the state introduced that included circled highlights and other changes that prosecutors said were designed to help viewers better understand the footage. Forensic testing for three key pieces of evidence became the focal point of Tuesday afternoon. Prosecutors called to the stand Jennifer Faumuina, a sergeant with the Utah department of public safety, to discuss the chain of custody and testing for a screwdriver found on a rooftop perch, a Mauser 98 rifle recovered from a wooden campus area and a towel used to wrap the firearm. Faumuina read aloud from an FBI report about the DNA of Robinson and his roommate, Lance Twiggs, being found on the towel. Michael Burt, the defense attorney, previewed a trial tactic where he might call into question the accuracy of the testing that found Robinson’s DNA on the towel. Burt asked Amanda Bakker, an FBI forensic analyst, about a 2004 report that found “a number of errors” with the way testing was done at her laboratory in Quantico, Virginia. “Objection, a 2004 study is hardly relevant to this proceeding, 22 years later,” Ryan McBride, a state prosecutor, interjected. “It’s relevant because he’s relying on accreditation to somehow say that these results are valid,” countered Burt. Graf allowed the line of questioning to briefly continue before court adjourned Tuesday afternoon. The hearing is scheduled to resume on Wednesday at 1pm local time. The Associated Press contributed reporting
chineseTitle: 检察官称视频显示查理·柯克杀人案嫌疑人爬上屋顶
chineseSummary: 犹他州检察官办公室表示，针对泰勒·詹姆斯·罗宾逊的证据足以使案件进入审判阶段
chineseContent: 犹他州检察官周二展示了一段视频，据称显示泰勒·詹姆斯·罗宾逊在查理·柯克被杀当天进入犹他谷大学校园，并爬上屋顶，据称他从那里向保守派评论员开了致命一枪。犹他州县检察官办公室在为期一周的初步听证会的第二天提交了证据，检察官必须向州地方法官托尼·格拉夫证明有足够的证据使案件进入审判阶段。 23 岁的罗宾逊因杀害柯克而被指控犯有严重谋杀罪，柯克去年 9 月在犹他大学演讲时颈部中枪身亡。检察官正在寻求对罗宾逊判处死刑，并表示有 DNA 证据表明他与据信杀人时使用的武器有关。该州还表示，罗宾逊在给室友和浪漫伴侣留下的纸条中承认了这一点。罗宾逊尚未对此案提出抗辩。本周的诉讼程序标志着柯克的家人和他的遗孀埃里卡·柯克第一次与被指控的凶手一起出庭。柯克的密友小唐纳德·特朗普周一也出席了会议。在听证会的第一天，前犹他谷大学警官克里斯托弗·巴格利 (Christopher Bagley) 描述了 2025 年 9 月 10 日柯克向数千人讲话时听到一声枪响，以及随之而来的恐慌。 “每个人都站了起来。很多人尖叫着，开始向各个不同的方向奔跑，”巴格利告诉法庭。他说，他搜寻了更多受害者，但一无所获，于是采取行动控制现场以保存证据。不久之后，巴格利检查了附近一座建筑物的屋顶，并在砾石中看到了他认为表明的痕迹
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
